content.assessment: This proposal exploits existing discretionary ministerial authority over state-sector capacity, which is a legally available and low-friction lever. However, its governance reach is structurally partial: the Klebelsberg Centre's jurisdiction explicitly excludes church, foundation, and private-sector maintainers, meaning any capacity contraction achieved administratively in the state sector has no legal purchase on the segment of the system most rapidly growing its selective share.
opportunities: The 2023 unified state employment framework across all provider types demonstrates a legal pathway by which cross-maintainer regulatory reach has been extended before, suggesting analogous extension of capacity-control authority is not without precedent (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
strengths: Uses existing discretionary approval power already exercised (denial of new 6/8-year track requests), meaning no new statute or constitutional test is required (F-live-legal_and_governance-07 supports the existence of a defined jurisdiction to act within)
threats: Legally partial contraction risks displacement of demand toward exempt maintainers, and because those maintainers operate under different accountability lines, this outcome could occur without any single authority being accountable for the net segregation effect (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
Hungary's already outlier levels of between-school variation suggest a partial legal lever is unlikely to meaningfully alter an entrenched governance-level pattern (F-live-legal_and_governance-06)
weaknesses: State-sector jurisdiction under Klebelsberg Centre and its 60 district sub-units explicitly excludes church, foundation, and purely private schools, creating a structural accountability gap since the lever cannot reach the fastest-growing selective segment (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)

Contraction of state capacity alone leaves the underlying admission criteria and rights framework unchanged, so the equity-per-seat problem persists in whatever seats remain (consistent with the broader segregation pattern documented in F-live-legal_and_governance-04)
